scope_note: QA To-Be-Prime / Euclid VII Prop 28 Coprimeness Chain cert [325]. Primary source: Iverson, B. (1991) Quantum Arithmetic Volume II -- Books 3 & 4: New Wave Theory / Synchronous Harmonics. Delta Spectrum Research, La Junta CO. pp.2-3, section 'TO BE PRIME'. Five structural results certified: (C1) Neighbor coprimeness: gcd(n,n+1)=1 for all n in {1..100} -- Iverson: 'Every integer is prime to any integer which is one unit less or one more than itself.' (C2) Odd skip-2 coprimeness: gcd(odd_n, odd_n+2)=1 for all odd n in {1..99}; gcd(2,4)=2 confirms Iverson's qualifier 'provided the original number is not divisible by two.' (C3) Euclid VII Prop 28 SUM: for all a,b in {1..50} with gcd(a,b)=1, gcd(a+b,a)=1 and gcd(a+b,b)=1; exhaustively verified ~1500 coprime pairs. (C4) BEDA coprimeness chain: for all mod-9 Cosmos pairs (b,e) with gcd(b,e)=1, Prop 28 applied twice gives gcd(d,b)=gcd(d,e)=1 (d=b+e raw) then gcd(a,d)=gcd(a,e)=1 (a=b+2e raw=d+e); this is the algebraic foundation of why the QA quadruple (b,e,d,a) consists of pairwise coprime integers when gcd(b,e)=1. (C5) Euclid VII Prop 28 DIFF: for all a>b in {1..50} with gcd(a,b)=1, gcd(a-b,a)=1 and gcd(a-b,b)=1; Iverson: 'the sum and the difference between two coprime integers will be prime to both of them.' First cert in QA-4 source series (Quantum Arithmetic Volume II, 1991). DOI: primary text, no external DOI.
